question:
Triangle $ABC$ has an area 1.  Points $E,F,G$ lie, respectively,
on sides $BC$, $CA$, $AB$ such that $AE$ bisects $BF$ at point $R$,
$BF$ bisects $CG$ at point $S$, and $CG$ bisects $AE$ at point $T$.
Find the area of the triangle $RST$.
solution:
Choose $r,s,t$ so that $EC = rBC, FA = sCA, GB = tCB$, and let
$[XYZ]$ denote the area of triangle $XYZ$. Then $[ABE] = [AFE]$ since
the triangles have the same altitude and base.
Also $[ABE] = (BE/BC) [ABC] = 1-r$, and
$[ECF] = (EC/BC)(CF/CA)[ABC] = r(1-s)$ (e.g., by the law of sines).
Adding this
all up yields
\begin{align*}
1 &= [ABE] + [ABF] + [ECF] \\
&= 2(1-r) + r(1-s) = 2-r-rs
\end{align*}
or $r(1+s) = 1$.
Similarly $s(1+t) = t(1+r) = 1$.

Let $f: [0, \infty) \to [0, \infty)$ be the function given by
$f(x) = 1/(1+x)$; then $f(f(f(r))) = r$.
However, $f(x)$ is strictly decreasing in $x$, so $f(f(x))$ is increasing
and $f(f(f(x)))$ is decreasing. Thus there is at most one $x$ such that
$f(f(f(x))) = x$;
in fact, since the equation $f(z) = z$ has a positive root
$z = (-1 + \sqrt{5})/2$, we must have $r=s=t=z$.

We now compute $[ABF] = (AF/AC) [ABC] = z$,
$[ABR] = (BR/BF) [ABF] = z/2$, analogously $[BCS] = [CAT] = z/2$, and
$[RST] = |[ABC] - [ABR] - [BCS] - [CAT]| = |1 - 3z/2| = \frac{7 - 3
\sqrt{5}}{4}$.

Note: the key relation $r(1+s) = 1$ can also be derived by computing
using homogeneous coordinates or vectors.

descriptive_long.question:
Triangle $vertexavertexbvertexc$ has an area 1.  Points $vertexe,vertexf,vertexg$ lie, respectively,
on sides $vertexbvertexc$, $vertexcvertexa$, $vertexavertexb$ such that $vertexavertexe$ bisects $vertexbvertexf$ at point $vertexr$,
$vertexbvertexf$ bisects $vertexcvertexg$ at point $vertexs$, and $vertexcvertexg$ bisects $vertexavertexe$ at point $vertext$.
Find the area of the triangle $vertexrvertexsvertext$.
descriptive_long.solution:
Choose $ratioone, ratiotwo, ratiothr$ so that $vertexevertexc = ratioone vertexbvertexc, vertexfvertexa = ratiotwo vertexcvertexa, vertexgvertexb = ratiothr vertexcvertexb$, and let
$[vertexxvertexyvertexz]$ denote the area of triangle $vertexxvertexyvertexz$. Then $[vertexavertexbvertexe] = [vertexavertexfvertexe]$ since
the triangles have the same altitude and base.
Also $[vertexavertexbvertexe] = (vertexbvertexe/vertexbvertexc) [vertexavertexbvertexc] = 1 - ratioone$, and
$[vertexevertexcvertexf] = (vertexevertexc/vertexbvertexc)(vertexcvertexf/vertexcvertexa)[vertexavertexbvertexc] = ratioone(1 - ratiotwo)$ (e.g., by the law of sines).
Adding this
all up yields
\begin{align*}
1 &= [vertexavertexbvertexe] + [vertexavertexbvertexf] + [vertexevertexcvertexf] \\
&= 2(1 - ratioone) + ratioone(1 - ratiotwo) = 2 - ratioone - ratioone ratiotwo
\end{align*}
or $ratioone(1 + ratiotwo) = 1$.
Similarly $ratiotwo(1 + ratiothr) = ratiothr(1 + ratioone) = 1$.

Let $mapfun: [0, \infty) \to [0, \infty)$ be the function given by
$mapfun(varxvar) = 1/(1 + varxvar)$; then $mapfun(mapfun(mapfun(ratioone))) = ratioone$.
However, $mapfun(varxvar)$ is strictly decreasing in $varxvar$, so $mapfun(mapfun(varxvar))$ is increasing
and $mapfun(mapfun(mapfun(varxvar)))$ is decreasing. Thus there is at most one $varxvar$ such that
$mapfun(mapfun(mapfun(varxvar))) = varxvar$;
in fact, since the equation $mapfun(varzvar) = varzvar$ has a positive root
$varzvar = (-1 + \sqrt{5})/2$, we must have $ratioone = ratiotwo = ratiothr = varzvar$.

We now compute $[vertexavertexbvertexf] = (vertexfvertexa/vertexavertexc) [vertexavertexbvertexc] = varzvar$,
$[vertexavertexbvertexr] = (vertexbvertexr/vertexbvertexf) [vertexavertexbvertexf] = varzvar/2$, analogously $[vertexbvertexcvertexs] = [vertexcvertexavertext] = varzvar/2$, and
$[vertexrvertexsvertext] = |[vertexavertexbvertexc] - [vertexavertexbvertexr] - [vertexbvertexcvertexs] - [vertexcvertexavertext]| = |1 - 3 varzvar/2| = \frac{7 - 3
\sqrt{5}}{4}$.

Note: the key relation $ratioone(1 + ratiotwo) = 1$ can also be derived by computing
using homogeneous coordinates or vectors.

descriptive_long_confusing.question:
Triangle $hummingbird copperhead windmill$ has an area 1.  Points $raincloud,dragonfly,parchment$ lie, respectively,
on sides $copperhead windmill$, $windmill hummingbird$, $hummingbird copperhead$ such that $hummingbird raincloud$ bisects $copperhead dragonfly$ at point $blueberry$,
$copperhead dragonfly$ bisects $windmill parchment$ at point $yellowtail$, and $windmill parchment$ bisects $hummingbird raincloud$ at point $peppercorn$.
Find the area of the triangle $blueberry yellowtail peppercorn$.
descriptive_long_confusing.solution:
Choose $sunflower,driftwood,blackbird$ so that $raincloud windmill = sunflower copperhead windmill$, $dragonfly hummingbird = driftwood windmill hummingbird$, $parchment copperhead = blackbird copperhead windmill$, and let
$[stoneware elderberry starflower]$ denote the area of triangle $[stoneware elderberry starflower]$. Then $[hummingbird copperhead raincloud] = [hummingbird dragonfly raincloud]$ since
the triangles have the same altitude and base.
Also $[hummingbird copperhead raincloud] = (copperhead raincloud / copperhead windmill) [hummingbird copperhead windmill] = 1-sunflower$, and
$[raincloud windmill dragonfly] = (raincloud windmill / copperhead windmill)(windmill dragonfly / windmill hummingbird)[hummingbird copperhead windmill] = sunflower(1-driftwood)$ (e.g., by the law of sines).
Adding this
all up yields
\\begin{align*}
1 &= [hummingbird copperhead raincloud] + [hummingbird copperhead dragonfly] + [raincloud windmill dragonfly] \\
&= 2(1-sunflower) + sunflower(1-driftwood) = 2-sunflower-sunflower driftwood
\\end{align*}
or $sunflower(1+driftwood) = 1$.
Similarly $driftwood(1+blackbird) = blackbird(1+sunflower) = 1$.

Let $silverware: [0, \\infty) \\to [0, \\infty)$ be the function given by
$silverware(meadowlark) = 1/(1+meadowlark)$; then $silverware(silverware(silverware(sunflower))) = sunflower$.
However, $silverware(meadowlark)$ is strictly decreasing in $meadowlark$, so $silverware(silverware(meadowlark))$ is increasing
and $silverware(silverware(silverware(meadowlark)))$ is decreasing. Thus there is at most one $meadowlark$ such that
$silverware(silverware(silverware(meadowlark))) = meadowlark$;
in fact, since the equation $silverware(candlewick) = candlewick$ has a positive root
$candlewick = (-1 + \\sqrt{5})/2$, we must have $sunflower = driftwood = blackbird = candlewick$.

We now compute $[hummingbird copperhead dragonfly] = (dragonfly hummingbird / hummingbird windmill) [hummingbird copperhead windmill] = candlewick$,
$[hummingbird copperhead blueberry] = (blueberry copperhead / copperhead dragonfly) [hummingbird copperhead dragonfly] = candlewick/2$, analogously $[copperhead windmill yellowtail] = [windmill hummingbird peppercorn] = candlewick/2$, and
$[blueberry yellowtail peppercorn] = |[hummingbird copperhead windmill] - [hummingbird copperhead blueberry] - [copperhead windmill yellowtail] - [windmill hummingbird peppercorn]| = |1 - 3candlewick/2| = \\frac{7 - 3
\\sqrt{5}}{4}$.

Note: the key relation $sunflower(1+driftwood) = 1$ can also be derived by computing
using homogeneous coordinates or vectors.

descriptive_long_misleading.question:
Triangle $voidpointgapcorneredgehole$ has an area 1.  Points $centerless,areawide,massivearea$ lie, respectively,
on sides $gapcorneredgehole$, $edgeholevoidpoint$, $voidpointgapcorner$ such that $voidpointcenterless$ bisects $gapcornerareawide$ at point $offcenter$,
$gapcornerareawide$ bisects $edgeholemassivearea$ at point $skewpoint$, and $edgeholemassivearea$ bisects $voidpointcenterless$ at point $disjointed$.
Find the area of the triangle $offcenterskewpointdisjointed$.
descriptive_long_misleading.solution:
Choose $wholevalue,entiresum,stillness$ so that $centerlessedgehole = wholevaluegapcorneredgehole, areawidevoidpoint = entiresumedgeholevoidpoint, massiveareagapcorner = stillnessedgeholegapcorner$, and let
$[unknowablecertaintyoriginless]$ denote the area of triangle $unknowablecertaintyoriginless$. Then $[voidpointgapcornercenterless] = [areawidevoidpointcenterless]$ since
the triangles have the same altitude and base.
Also $[voidpointgapcornercenterless] = (gapcornercenterless/gapcorneredgehole) [voidpointgapcorneredgehole] = 1-wholevalue$, and
$[centerlessedgeholeareawide] = (centerlessedgehole/gapcorneredgehole)(centerlessareawide/edgeholevoidpoint)[voidpointgapcorneredgehole] = wholevalue(1-entiresum)$ (e.g., by the law of sines).
Adding this
all up yields
\begin{align*}
1 &= [voidpointgapcornercenterless] + [voidpointgapcornerareawide] + [centerlessedgeholeareawide] \\
&= 2(1-wholevalue) + wholevalue(1-entiresum) = 2-wholevalue-wholevalueentiresum
\end{align*}
or $wholevalue(1+entiresum) = 1$.
Similarly $entiresum(1+stillness) = stillness(1+wholevalue) = 1$.

Let $immutability: [0, \infty) \to [0, \infty)$ be the function given by
$immutability(fixedvalue) = 1/(1+fixedvalue)$; then $immutability(immutability(immutability(wholevalue))) = wholevalue$.
However, $immutability(fixedvalue)$ is strictly decreasing in $fixedvalue$, so $immutability(immutability(fixedvalue))$ is increasing
and $immutability(immutability(immutability(fixedvalue)))$ is decreasing. Thus there is at most one $fixedvalue$ such that
$immutability(immutability(immutability(fixedvalue))) = fixedvalue$;
in fact, since the equation $immutability(nadirpoint) = nadirpoint$ has a positive root
$nadirpoint = (-1 + \sqrt{5})/2$, we must have $wholevalue=entiresum=stillness=nadirpoint$.

We now compute $[voidpointgapcornerareawide] = (areawidevoidpoint/voidpointedgehole) [voidpointgapcorneredgehole] = nadirpoint$,
$[voidpointgapcorneroffcenter] = (gapcorneroffcenter/gapcornerareawide) [voidpointgapcornerareawide] = nadirpoint/2$, analogously $[gapcorneredgeholeskewpoint] = [edgeholevoidpointdisjointed] = nadirpoint/2$, and
$[offcenterskewpointdisjointed] = |[voidpointgapcorneredgehole] - [voidpointgapcorneroffcenter] - [gapcorneredgeholeskewpoint] - [edgeholevoidpointdisjointed]| = |1 - 3nadirpoint/2| = \frac{7 - 3
\sqrt{5}}{4}$.

Note: the key relation $wholevalue(1+entiresum) = 1$ can also be derived by computing
using homogeneous coordinates or vectors.

garbled_string.question:
Triangle $lkjhgfdsapoiuytrewmnbvcxzlk$ has an area 1.  Points $asdfghjkl,qazwsxedc,edcrfvtgb$ lie, respectively,
on sides $poiuytrewmnbvcxzlk$, $mnbvcxzlklkjhgfdsa$, $lkjhgfdsapoiuytrew$ such that $lkjhgfdsaasdfghjkl$ bisects $poiuytrewqazwsxedc$ at point $yhnujmiko$,
$poiuytrewqazwsxedc$ bisects $mnbvcxzlkedcrfvtgb$ at point $olpkiujmy$, and $mnbvcxzlkedcrfvtgb$ bisects $lkjhgfdsaasdfghjkl$ at point $ujmnhytre$.
Find the area of the triangle $yhnujmikoolpkiujmyujmnhytre$.
garbled_string.solution:
Choose $qzxwvtnp,hjgrksla,mnfdciou$ so that $asdfghjklmnbvcxzlk=qzxwvtnppoiuytrewmnbvcxzlk$, $qazwsxedclkjhgfdsa=hjgrkslamnbvcxzlklkjhgfdsa$, and $edcrfvtgbpoiuytrew=mnfdcioumnbvcxzlkpoiuytrew$, and let $[ikmjnhbgttrewqasdfplkmijnuh]$ denote the area of triangle $ikmjnhbgttrewqasdfplkmijnuh$. Then $[lkjhgfdsapoiuytrewasdfghjkl]=[lkjhgfdsaqazwsxedcasdfghjkl]$ since the triangles have the same altitude and base.
Also $[lkjhgfdsapoiuytrewasdfghjkl]=(poiuytrewasdfghjkl/poiuytrewmnbvcxzlk)[lkjhgfdsapoiuytrewmnbvcxzlk]=1-qzxwvtnp$, and $[asdfghjklmnbvcxzlkqazwsxedc]=(asdfghjklmnbvcxzlk/poiuytrewmnbvcxzlk)(mnbvcxzlkqazwsxedc/mnbvcxzlklkjhgfdsa)[lkjhgfdsapoiuytrewmnbvcxzlk]=qzxwvtnp(1-hjgrksla)$ (e.g., by the law of sines).
Adding this all up yields
\begin{align*}
1&=[lkjhgfdsapoiuytrewasdfghjkl]+[lkjhgfdsapoiuytrewqazwsxedc]+[asdfghjklmnbvcxzlkqazwsxedc]\\
&=2(1-qzxwvtnp)+qzxwvtnp(1-hjgrksla)=2-qzxwvtnp-qzxwvtnphjgrksla
\end{align*}
or $qzxwvtnp(1+hjgrksla)=1$.  Similarly $hjgrksla(1+mnfdciou)=mnfdciou(1+qzxwvtnp)=1$.

Let $wsxzaqwer:[0,\infty)\to[0,\infty)$ be the function given by $wsxzaqwer(plokmijn)=1/(1+plokmijn)$; then $wsxzaqwer(wsxzaqwer(wsxzaqwer(qzxwvtnp)))=qzxwvtnp$.
However, $wsxzaqwer(plokmijn)$ is strictly decreasing in $plokmijn$, so $wsxzaqwer(wsxzaqwer(plokmijn))$ is increasing and $wsxzaqwer(wsxzaqwer(wsxzaqwer(plokmijn)))$ is decreasing. Thus there is at most one $plokmijn$ such that $wsxzaqwer(wsxzaqwer(wsxzaqwer(plokmijn)))=plokmijn$; in fact, since the equation $wsxzaqwer(bvcxzasd)=bvcxzasd$ has a positive root $bvcxzasd=(-1+\sqrt{5})/2$, we must have $qzxwvtnp=hjgrksla=mnfdciou=bvcxzasd$.

We now compute $[lkjhgfd sapoiuytrewqazwsxedc]=(qazwsxedclkjhgfdsa/lkjhgfdsa mnbvcxzlk)[lkjhgfdsapoiuytrewmnbvcxzlk]=bvcxzasd$, $[lkjhgfdsapoiuytrewyhnujmiko]=(yhnujmikopoiuytrew/poiuytrewqazwsxedc)[lkjhgfd sapoiuytrewqazwsxedc]=bvcxzasd/2$, analogously $[poiuytrewmnbvcxzlk olpkiujmy]=[mnbvcxzlklkjhgfdsa ujmnhytre]=bvcxzasd/2$, and $[yhnujmikoolpkiujmyujmnhytre]=|[lkjhgfdsapoiuytrewmnbvcxzlk]-[lkjhgfdsapoiuytrewyhnujmiko]-[poiuytrewmnbvcxzlk olpkiujmy]-[mnbvcxzlklkjhgfdsa ujmnhytre]|=|1-3bvcxzasd/2|=\frac{7-3\sqrt{5}}{4}$.

Note: the key relation $qzxwvtnp(1+hjgrksla)=1$ can also be derived by computing using homogeneous coordinates or vectors.

kernel_variant.question:
Triangle \(PQR\) has area \(60\).  Points \(U,\;V,\;W\) are chosen on sides \(QR,\;RP,\;PQ\), respectively.  
Let  
\[
M=PU\cap QV,\qquad   
N=QV\cap RW,\qquad   
O=RW\cap PU .
\]

These three intersection points satisfy  
(1) \(M\) divides \(QV\) internally in the ratio \(QM:MV=1:2\);  
(2) \(N\) divides \(RW\) internally in the ratio \(RN:NW=1:2\);  
(3) \(O\) divides \(PU\) internally in the ratio \(PO:OU=1:2\).

Determine the exact area of triangle \(MNO\).
kernel_variant.solution:
Step 1.  Notation and barycentric coordinates  
Put triangle \(PQR\) in barycentric coordinates relative to itself, so
\[
P=(1,0,0),\;Q=(0,1,0),\;R=(0,0,1),\qquad
\text{and }[PQR]=1.
\]
(We will restore the given area \(60\) at the very end.)

Write 
\[
U=(0,1-u,u),\qquad 
V=(v,0,1-v),\qquad 
W=(1-w,w,0),\qquad 0<u,v,w<1,
\]
where, for example, \(u=QU/QR\).

Step 2.  Barycentric coordinates of \(M\)  
Because \(M\in PU\), it can be written
\[
M=(1-s,\,s(1-u),\,su) \qquad(0<s<1).
\]
Because \(M\in QV\) and \(QM:MV=1:2\), the point divides \(QV\) in the
ratio \(1:2\), i.e.\ its parameter on \(QV\) is \(t=\tfrac13\):
\[
M=(tv,\,1-t,\,t(1-v))\;=\;\Bigl(\tfrac{v}{3},\,\tfrac23,\,
\tfrac{1-v}{3}\Bigr).
\]

Equating the two expressions for \(M\) gives
\[
\begin{cases}
1-s=\dfrac{v}{3},\\[4pt]
s(1-u)=\dfrac23,\\[6pt]
su=\dfrac{1-v}{3}.
\end{cases}\tag{1}
\]

Step 3.  Solving (1) for \(v\) and \(s\) in terms of \(u\)  
From the second equation \(s=\dfrac{2}{3(1-u)}\).
Plugging this into the first gives
\[
1-\frac{2}{3(1-u)}=\frac{v}{3}\quad\Longrightarrow\quad
v=3-\frac{2}{1-u}. \tag{2}
\]

Step 4.  Analogue for \(N\)  
Rotating the role of the letters \(P,Q,R\) yields, from the
requirements for \(N\),
\[
w=3-\frac{2}{1-v}. \tag{3}
\]

Step 5.  Analogue for \(O\)  
Rotating once more gives, from the requirements for \(O\),
\[
u=3-\frac{2}{1-w}. \tag{4}
\]

Step 6.  Reducing to one equation  
Equations (2)-(4) may be read as
\[
v=f(u),\qquad w=f(v),\qquad u=f(w),
\quad\text{where }f(x)=3-\dfrac{2}{1-x}.
\]
Hence \(u\) satisfies \(f(f(f(u)))=u\).
Since  
\(f\) is strictly decreasing and \(f\!\circ f\) is strictly increasing,
\(f\!\circ f\!\circ f\) is strictly decreasing, so the functional
equation has at most one solution in \((0,1)\).  
But \(f(x)=x\) gives the quadratic
\[
x=3-\frac{2}{1-x}\;\Longrightarrow\;x^2-4x+1=0
\;\Longrightarrow\;
x=2-\sqrt3\in(0,1).
\]
Hence the only possible consistent choice is
\[
u=v=w=2-\sqrt3. \tag{5}
\]

Step 7.  Exact barycentric coordinates of the three points  
First compute \(s\) from (1) and (5):
\[
s=\frac{2}{3(1-u)}
=\frac{2}{3(\sqrt3-1)}
=\frac{\sqrt3+1}{3}.
\]
Therefore
\[
\begin{aligned}
M&=\bigl(1-s,\;s(1-u),\;su\bigr)                                   \\
  &=\Bigl(\underbrace{\dfrac{2-\sqrt3}{3}}_{=:a},\;
          \underbrace{\dfrac{2}{3}}_{=:b},\;
          \underbrace{\dfrac{\sqrt3-1}{3}}_{=:c}\Bigr).
\end{aligned}
\]
By cyclic symmetry,
\[
N=(c,a,b),\qquad O=(b,c,a).
\]

Step 8.  Area ratio via a determinant  
For three points with barycentric coordinates 
\((\alpha_i,\beta_i,\gamma_i)\), \(i=1,2,3\),
their area equals \([PQR]\cdot|\det(\alpha_i,\beta_i,\gamma_i)|\).
Hence
\[
\frac{[MNO]}{[PQR]}
=\left|
\begin{vmatrix}
a&b&c\\
c&a&b\\
b&c&a
\end{vmatrix}
\right|.
\]
Because the matrix is circulant, its determinant equals
\[
(a+b+c)\bigl(a^2+b^2+c^2-ab-bc-ca\bigr).
\]
Now \(a+b+c=1\), so
\[
\det= a^2+b^2+c^2-ab-bc-ca.
\]
Insert the exact values
\(
a=\dfrac{2-\sqrt3}{3},\;
b=\dfrac{2}{3},\;
c=\dfrac{\sqrt3-1}{3}
\)
to obtain
\[
\det
=\frac{18-9\sqrt3}{9}=2-\sqrt3.
\]
Hence
\[
[MNO]=(2-\sqrt3)\,[PQR].
\]

Step 9.  Restoring the given area  
Given \([PQR]=60\), we finally get
\[
[MNO]=60\,(2-\sqrt3)=\boxed{\,120-60\sqrt3\,}.
\]

\begin{aligned}

\[
(a+b+c)\bigl(a^2+b^2+c^2-ab-bc-ca\bigr).
\]
Now \(a+b+c=1\), so
\[
\det= a^2+b^2+c^2-ab-bc-ca.
\]
Insert the exact values
\(
a=\dfrac{2-\sqrt3}{3},\;
b=\dfrac{2}{3},\;
c=\dfrac{\sqrt3-1}{3}
\)
to obtain
\[
\det
=\frac{18-9\sqrt3}{9}=2-\sqrt3.
\]
Hence
\[
[MNO]=(2-\sqrt3)\,[PQR].
\]

Step 9.  Restoring the given area  
Given \([PQR]=60\), we finally get
\[
[MNO]=60\,(2-\sqrt3)=\boxed{\,120-60\sqrt3\,}.
\]
